#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

Closes https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21660

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->

It removes the usage of `ld_classic` flag on macOS. I tested locally on my system (macOS with `Apple clang version 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.4)`) and on CI (see https://github.com/czgdp1807/scipy/pull/10). It works fine and doesn't give any warnings. Tests also pass.

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

Describe the issue:

Arithmetic operators like division do not take shape typing into account, resulting in false-positive type hinting issues.

Originally reported here: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27957#issuecomment-2530265613
Some debugging work done by @jorenham at here: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27957#issuecomment-2531559754
Opening a separate bug report as requested

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

x = np.zeros(1)
x = x / 1
```


### Error message:

```shell
test.py:4: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.2.0
3.13.0 (main, Nov 21 2024, 10:46:46) [Clang 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.4)]

### Type-checker version and settings:

mypy 1.11.2 (compiled: no)
mypy --strict

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [x] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

According to the feedstock, numpy 1.20.1+ is supported in torchmetrics 1.5.1. However, according to [here](https://github.com/Lightning-AI/torchmetrics/blame/v1.5.1/requirements/base.txt), numpy 2 is not supported. This [should be fixed](https://github.com/Lightning-AI/torchmetrics/pull/2804) in the next torchmetrics release, but it would be nice to make sure `pip check` passes for this feedstock as well.

I'm trying to add a new package for [lightning-uq-box](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/pull/27972) but it's failing `pip check` because torchmetrics does not properly specify its dependencies. I either have to remove `pip check` or add a `<2` constraint to numpy, which isn't true for my package.

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [x] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

According to the feedstock, numpy 1.20.1+ is supported in torchmetrics 1.5.1. However, according to [here](https://github.com/Lightning-AI/torchmetrics/blame/v1.5.1/requirements/base.txt), numpy 2 is not supported. This [should be fixed](https://github.com/Lightning-AI/torchmetrics/pull/2804) in the next torchmetrics release, but it would be nice to make sure `pip check` passes for this feedstock as well.

I'm trying to add a new package for [lightning-uq-box](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/pull/27972) but it's failing `pip check` because torchmetrics does not properly specify its dependencies. I either have to remove `pip check` or add a `<2` constraint to numpy, which isn't true for my package.

It looks like jsonnet 0.13+ exists, just not for Windows: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/jsonnet/files

I think you'll find that it is becoming increasingly difficult to use Windows for PyTorch development. The conda-forge PyTorch recipe [does not support Windows](https://github.com/conda-forge/pytorch-cpu-feedstock/issues/32) at the moment. The official pytorch conda channel [is now deprecated](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/138506) as well.

My honest suggestion would be to avoid conda, at least on Windows. TorchGeo supports conda installation as best we can, but we can't control what our dependencies do. I think you'll have more luck with pip, and I generally recommend pip for the vast majority of users. I would also recommend Spack, but its Windows support is still very preliminary.

Your other option is to avoid Windows. Most developers I know use Bash for Windows so that they can install Linux-compatible PyTorch installations.

Sorry I couldn't give a better solution here.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/torchgeo-feedstock/issues/comments/2557778874/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

tight_layout function is very useful.

In one of the common use cases, a figure with a few subplots, it would be nice if a figure title could be taken into account; otherwise it will overlap the top subplots.

I can see from the code that the suptitle is just a simple Text; maybe the figure could keep track of the last Text object that got created using suptitle(), and so be able to take it into account when calculating the tight layout params.

tight_layout function is very useful.

In one of the common use cases, a figure with a few subplots, it would be nice if a figure title could be taken into account; otherwise it will overlap the top subplots.

I can see from the code that the suptitle is just a simple Text; maybe the figure could keep track of the last Text object that got created using suptitle(), and so be able to take it into account when calculating the tight layout params.

Bug summary

I am using [uv](https://github.com/astral-sh/uv). When upgrade from 3.9.2 to 3.9.3 in Python 3.12, I got error in [the build](https://github.com/hongbo-miao/hongbomiao.com/actions/runs/12095612472/job/33729155287?pr=20940).

### Code for reproduction

```Python
[project]
name = "hm-test"
version = "1.0.0"
requires-python = "~=3.12.0"
dependencies = [
    "matplotlib==3.9.3"
]
```

Then run `uv sync`.

### Actual outcome

> Distribution `matplotlib==3.9.3 @ registry+[https://pypi.org/simple`](https://pypi.org/simple%60) can't be installed because it doesn't have a source distribution or wheel for the current platform

### Expected outcome

I expect matplotlib 3.9.3 can be installed in Python 3.12.

### Additional information

matplotlib 3.9.3 can be installed successfully in Python 3.13: [build](https://github.com/hongbo-miao/hongbomiao.com/actions/runs/12095694254/job/33729165842).

### Operating system

Ubuntu

### Matplotlib Version

3.9.3

### Matplotlib Backend

_No response_

### Python version

3.12.0

### Jupyter version

_No response_

### Installation

#### Reference Issues/PRs
<!--
Example: Fixes #1234. See also #3456.

sample_weight support will contributes to class_weight support, so this PR contributes to the issue https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/9113, and the stalled PR https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/11723 

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

- [x] Add sample_weight support for MLP

 

   - [x] Add sample_weight support for multiple loss functions:
     - [x] log_loss
     - [x] binary_log_loss
     - [x]  squared_loss
   - [x]  Add sample_weight parameter to fit and partial_fit
     - [x]  Propagate to relevant functions
     - [x]  Apply sample_weight to gradient calculation
     - [x]  Validate sample_weight
     - [x]  Handle zero weights
   - [x]  Add tests
     - [x]  Test directly loss functions sample_weight invariance
     - [x]  Test fit validation of sample_weight
     - [x]  Test sample weight effect for both MLPClassifier and MLPRegressor
     - [x] Test sample weight together with early stopping


#### Any other comments? 
I have a draft PR https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/25326 to support class weight for MLP which will depend on this PR for sample weight support first.

sample_weight support will contributes to class_weight support, so this PR contributes to the issue https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/9113, and the stalled PR https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/11723 

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

- [x] Add sample_weight support for MLP

 

   - [x] Add sample_weight support for multiple loss functions:
     - [x] log_loss
     - [x] binary_log_loss
     - [x]  squared_loss
   - [x]  Add sample_weight parameter to fit and partial_fit
     - [x]  Propagate to relevant functions
     - [x]  Apply sample_weight to gradient calculation
     - [x]  Validate sample_weight
     - [x]  Handle zero weights
   - [x]  Add tests
     - [x]  Test directly loss functions sample_weight invariance
     - [x]  Test fit validation of sample_weight
     - [x]  Test sample weight effect for both MLPClassifier and MLPRegressor
     - [x] Test sample weight together with early stopping


#### Any other comments? 
I have a draft PR https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/25326 to support class weight for MLP which will depend on this PR for sample weight support first.

MLP","user":{"login":"zshu115x","id":14339180,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0MzM5MTgw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/14339180?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x","html_url":"https://github.com/zshu115x","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zshu115x/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"<!--
Thanks for contributing a pull request! Please ensure you have taken a look at
the contribution guidelines: https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md
-->

#### Reference Issues/PRs
<!--
Example: Fixes #1234. See also #3456.
Please use keywords (e.g., Fixes) to create link to the issues or pull requests
you resolved, so that they will automatically be closed when your pull request
is merged. See https://github.com/blog/1506-closing-issues-via-pull-requests
-->
sample_weight support will contributes to class_weight support, so this PR contributes to the issue https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/9113, and the stalled PR https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/11723 

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.

- [x] Add sample_weight support for MLP

 

   - [x] Add sample_weight support for multiple loss functions:
     - [x] log_loss
     - [x] binary_log_loss
     - [x]  squared_loss
   - [x]  Add sample_weight parameter to fit and partial_fit
     - [x]  Propagate to relevant functions
     - [x]  Apply sample_weight to gradient calculation
     - [x]  Validate sample_weight
     - [x]  Handle zero weights
   - [x]  Add tests
     - [x]  Test directly loss functions sample_weight invariance
     - [x]  Test fit validation of sample_weight
     - [x]  Test sample weight effect for both MLPClassifier and MLPRegressor
     - [x] Test sample weight together with early stopping


#### Any other comments? 
I have a draft PR https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/pull/25326 to support class weight for MLP which will depend on this PR for sample weight support first.

Using the following code from [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/subplots_axes_and_figures/subplots_demo.html):
```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 400)
y = np.sin(x ** 2)
fig, axs = plt.subplots(2)  # or nrows=2
fig.suptitle('Vertically stacked subplots')
axs[0].plot(x, y)
axs[1].plot(x, -y)
```
mypy complains with:
```console
> mypy test.py
test.py:8: error: Value of type "Axes | ndarray[Any, Any]" is not indexable  [index]
test.py:9: error: Value of type "Axes | ndarray[Any, Any]" is not indexable  [index]
Found 2 errors in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```
There seems to be an issue with the overload. If we can figure that out, great. If not, we will have to revert back to `Any` and treat this function as being dynamic (static type analysis is not possible).

How did I notice this? We use multiple subplots quite heavily in [TorchGeo](https://github.com/microsoft/torchgeo). The PR that updated matplotlib from 3.9.0 to 3.9.1 results in [400+ mypy errors](https://github.com/microsoft/torchgeo/actions/runs/9799492149/job/27059832597?pr=2152) as a result of this PR. We _could_ add type hints to all 400+ locations to clarify that it is indeed a numpy array, but given that we're talking about 400+ locations, I would much rather fix this in 1 location in matplotlib instead.

P.S. Let me know if you want me to open a formal issue for this. Fixes https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/27001#issuecomment-2211785864

My solution is to have 3 possible cases:

1. `squeeze=True`, `nrows=1`, `ncols=1`: `Axes`
2. `squeeze=True`: `np.ndarray`
3. `squeeze=False`: `np.ndarray`

1 and 3 already existed. 2 is _hopefully_ treated as a fallback by mypy for when either `nrows` or `ncols` is not 1. All of my testing with `reveal_type` works as expected, but I would love suggestions for how to formally test this in CI so it doesn't happen again.

PR summary

Fixes https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/27001#issuecomment-2211785864

My solution is to have 3 possible cases:

1. `squeeze=True`, `nrows=1`, `ncols=1`: `Axes`
2. `squeeze=True`: `np.ndarray`
3. `squeeze=False`: `np.ndarray`

1 and 3 already existed. 2 is _hopefully_ treated as a fallback by mypy for when either `nrows` or `ncols` is not 1. All of my testing with `reveal_type` works as expected, but I would love suggestions for how to formally test this in CI so it doesn't happen again.

PR summary

Fixes https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/27001#issuecomment-2211785864

My solution is to have 3 possible cases:

1. `squeeze=True`, `nrows=1`, `ncols=1`: `Axes`
2. `squeeze=True`: `np.ndarray`
3. `squeeze=False`: `np.ndarray`

1 and 3 already existed. 2 is _hopefully_ treated as a fallback by mypy for when either `nrows` or `ncols` is not 1. All of my testing with `reveal_type` works as expected, but I would love suggestions for how to formally test this in CI so it doesn't happen again.

## PR checklist

- [x] "closes #0000" is in the body of the PR description to [link the related issue](https://docs.github.com/en/issues/tracking-your-work-with-issues/linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue)
- [ ] new and changed code is [tested](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/testing.html)
- [ ] *Plotting related* features are demonstrated in an [example](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/document.html#write-examples-and-tutorials)
- [ ] *New Features* and *API Changes* are noted with a [directive and release note](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/api_changes.html#announce-changes-deprecations-and-new-features)
- [ ] Documentation complies with [general](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/document.html#write-rest-pages) and [docstring](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/document.html#write-docstrings) PR summary

Fixes https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/27001#issuecomment-2211785864

My solution is to have 3 possible cases:

1. `squeeze=True`, `nrows=1`, `ncols=1`: `Axes`
2. `squeeze=True`: `np.ndarray`
3. `squeeze=False`: `np.ndarray`

1 and 3 already existed. 2 is _hopefully_ treated as a fallback by mypy for when either `nrows` or `ncols` is not 1. All of my testing with `reveal_type` works as expected, but I would love suggestions for how to formally test this in CI so it doesn't happen again.

I think we may be able to get by by simply changing only the "fallback" case from having a union return type to just being `Any`, but keeping the more specific versions to provide the benefits of having type hints when we can.

Thanks, I think this is a better solution than what I came up with.

> The truth is that the type hint is _not_ wrong...

This is where I disagree.

> we cannot say whether it will be a single Axes or an ndarray based on the type...

This is referred to as _dynamic typing_. In Python [Any](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#the-any-type) is used "to indicate that a value is dynamically typed." Using a union as a return type implies that the return value can be of either type, _and that all uses of that function must be able to handle both_. Unions are almost never used in return types, only in parameter types.[^1][^2]

[^1]: https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/1693
[^2]: https://github.com/python/typing/issues/566","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2222334373/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe the workflow you want to enable

`GridSearchCV` supports multiple scoring metrics using the `scoring` parameter. However, this only applies to `fit`, not to `score`. I would like to use these same scoring metrics to evaluate on the test set as well.

### Describe your proposed solution

This change would require changing the return type of `score` depending on whether `scoring` is a list of metrics. Other than that, it should be easy to iterate over `scorer_` and compute the test accuracy on the same set of metrics used during scoring.

### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

The only alternative right now is to manually loop over `scorer_` and compute the metrics yourself using `predict` instead of `score`.

### Additional context

As an example, the last line in the following code block should return scores for all `scoring` metrics:
```python
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, train_test_split

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y)

estimator = LogisticRegression(n_jobs=-1)
param_grid = {"C": [10, 1.0, 0.1]}
score = ["accuracy", "balanced_accuracy", "precision_macro", "recall_macro", "f1_macro"]
grid_search = GridSearchCV(estimator, param_grid, scoring=score, refit="accuracy")
grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)
grid_search.score(X_test, y_test)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29065/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29065/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Describe the workflow you want to enable

I want to enable non-accuracy metrics to ``estimator.score``, and ultimately deprecate the default values of ``accuracy`` and ``r2``. I would call it ``scoring`` though it's a bit redundant but consistent.
That would allow us to get rid of the default scoring methods, which are objectively bad and misleading, and it would require the minimum amount of code changes for anyone. 

### Describe your proposed solution

Replace

```python
est.score(X, y)
```
with
```python
est.score(X, y, scoring="accuracy")
```
or rather
```python
est.score(X, y, scoring="recall_macro")
```
(or ``r2`` for regression).

### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

- Keep current status, which is bad (both ``accuracy`` and ``r2`` are bad)
- Remove ``scoring`` method.

I can't think of any other alternatives tbh.

### Additional context

I think in theory this requires a slep, as it's changing shared API, right?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28995/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28995/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2122718356","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28995#issuecomment-2122718356","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/28995","id":2122718356,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAzd1s5-hhyU","user":{"login":"adamjstewart","id":12021217,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMDIxMjE3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12021217?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart","html_url":"https://github.com/adamjstewart","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-21T14:04:52Z","updated_at":"2024-05-21T14:04:52Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Related discussion in #29065. I'm using the `scoring` parameter for `GridSearchCV`, but currently it only applies to `fit`, not to `score`. I would like to be able to compute the same metrics for both. Example code looks like:
```python
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, train_test_split

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y)

estimator = LogisticRegression(n_jobs=-1)
param_grid = {"C": [10, 1.0, 0.1]}
score = ["accuracy", "balanced_accuracy", "precision_macro", "recall_macro", "f1_macro"]
grid_search = GridSearchCV(estimator, param_grid, scoring=score, refit="accuracy")
grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)
grid_search.score(X_test, y_test)
```
At the moment, `score` only computes accuracy. If I want to compute the same metrics as fit, I have to replace the last line with:
```python
metrics = {}
for name, scorer in grid_search.scorer_.items():
    score = scorer(grid_search, X_test, y_test)
    metrics[name] = score
```
IMO, sklearn is all about syntactic sugar. The fact that I can perform model training, cross validation, hyperparameter tuning, and refitting on a single line is awesome. The fact that evaluating on test takes 4 lines is a bit of a surprise.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2122718356/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

## PR summary

Tests and references images are stored in subdirectories for each toolkit.

## PR checklist

- [ ] "closes #0000" is in the body of the PR description to [link the related issue](https://docs.github.com/en/issues/tracking-your-work-with-issues/linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue)
- [ ] new and changed code is [tested](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/testing.html)
- [ ] *Plotting related* features are demonstrated in an [example](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/document.html#write-examples-and-tutorials)
- [ ] *New Features* and *API Changes* are noted with a [directive and release note](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/api_changes.html#announce-changes-deprecations-and-new-features)
- [ ] Documentation complies with [general](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/document.html#write-rest-pages) and [docstring](https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/document.html#write-docstrings) guidelines

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26001","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26001/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26001/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26001/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26001","id":2181682521,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86CCdVZ","number":26001,"title":"ENH: np.info = np.iinfo + np.finfo","user":{"login":"adamjstewart","id":12021217,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMDIxMjE3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12021217?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart","html_url":"https://github.com/adamjstewart","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-03-12T13:53:06Z","updated_at":"2024-03-12T13:53:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

Given a dtype, if I want to know the max values of that dtype, I currently need an if-statement of the form:
```python
if np.isdtype(dtype, 'integral'):
    max = np.iinfo(dtype).max
else:
    max = np.finfo(dtype).max
```
I propose to have a single `np.info` method that dispatches to the submethod based on type:
```python
max = np.info(dtype).max
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26001/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26001/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/2126","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/2126/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/2126/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/2126/events","html_url":"https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/2126","id":2197809603,"node_id":"I_kwDOAgGDcc6C_-nD","number":2126,"title":"Compatibility with pandoc version on ReadTheDocs","user":{"login":"adamjstewart","id":12021217,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMDIxMjE3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12021217?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart","html_url":"https://github.com/adamjstewart","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-03-20T15:19:43Z","updated_at":"2024-03-21T12:37:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The newest version of Ubuntu available on ReadTheDocs is Ubuntu 22.04, which comes with pandoc 2.9.2.1. However, nbconvert does not support this version of pandoc, resulting in the following (harmless) warning:
```
/home/docs/checkouts/readthedocs.org/user_builds/torchgeo/envs/1903/lib/python3.12/site-packages/nbsphinx/__init__.py:1058: RuntimeWarning: You are using an unsupported version of pandoc (2.9.2.1).
Your version must be at least (2.14.2) but less than (4.0.0).
Refer to https://pandoc.org/installing.html.
Continuing with doubts...
  nbconvert.utils.pandoc.check_pandoc_version()
```
It looks like the lower bound was changed in #1997, but I don't actually see any changes that require newer versions of pandoc. Is it possible to re-enable support for older versions of pandoc? At least until a newer version of pandoc becomes available on Ubuntu?

I wanted to document something that took me a long time to track down in case it affects anyone else. It's not likely but ...

We run our ci using gitlab and use docker-machine to scale the workers up and down. We use sysbox to provide docker in docker support securely as the docker runc.

I have spent probably over 40 hours to discover that installing py-numpy using spack when in a docker-container using sysbox 0.5.2-0, errors in a way that looks completely unrelated.

For example:

Expected behavior and actual behavior.

`GDAL` cannot link against arbitrary `proj` versions and will use `proj9` over any other `proj >=6`, regardless of how the search paths for cmake are constructed.

Specifically:
1. The documentation suggests that an arbitrary proj location can be specified via these cmake variables `PROJ_INCLUDE_DIR` and `PROJ_LIBRARY_RELEASE`
https://gdal.org/development/building_from_source.html#proj
2. However, these appear to only be used when searching for `proj` in a non-`CONFIG` mode
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/modules/packages/FindPROJ.cmake#L37
which only seems to happen when looking for `proj6`
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L378
3. Otherwise, the proj find logic uses a `CONFIG` search, and prioritizes `proj9` and `proj8` 
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L371

Therefore, the outcome is that arbitrary versions of proj>=6 cannot be used. 
For example, in the context of library dependency management like `spack`, if `proj7` is first in the cmake search paths, `GDAL` will pass over it, and use `proj 9`. This is problematic if a system `proj9` is used (e.g., from homebrew or apt) instead of a custom-built `proj`.

Below is a `find_pacakge` debug  trace from cmake where I was expecting a custom-built `proj7.2.1` to be used and linked against. Instead, `GDAL` linked against homebrew's `proj9`. The path `/Users/cmarsh/Documents/science/code/spack/opt/spack/darwin-ventura-m1/apple-clang-14.0.3/proj-7.2.1-ulvikgu6cuz3c2xwmuxifqlhnnkg6353/lib/cmake/proj/proj-config.cmake` was manually confirmed as valid.

Expected behavior and actual behavior.

`GDAL` cannot link against arbitrary `proj` versions and will use `proj9` over any other `proj >=6`, regardless of how the search paths for cmake are constructed.

Specifically:
1. The documentation suggests that an arbitrary proj location can be specified via these cmake variables `PROJ_INCLUDE_DIR` and `PROJ_LIBRARY_RELEASE`
https://gdal.org/development/building_from_source.html#proj
2. However, these appear to only be used when searching for `proj` in a non-`CONFIG` mode
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/modules/packages/FindPROJ.cmake#L37
which only seems to happen when looking for `proj6`
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L378
3. Otherwise, the proj find logic uses a `CONFIG` search, and prioritizes `proj9` and `proj8` 
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L371

Therefore, the outcome is that arbitrary versions of proj>=6 cannot be used. 
For example, in the context of library dependency management like `spack`, if `proj7` is first in the cmake search paths, `GDAL` will pass over it, and use `proj 9`. This is problematic if a system `proj9` is used (e.g., from homebrew or apt) instead of a custom-built `proj`.

Below is a `find_pacakge` debug  trace from cmake where I was expecting a custom-built `proj7.2.1` to be used and linked against. Instead, `GDAL` linked against homebrew's `proj9`. The path `/Users/cmarsh/Documents/science/code/spack/opt/spack/darwin-ventura-m1/apple-clang-14.0.3/proj-7.2.1-ulvikgu6cuz3c2xwmuxifqlhnnkg6353/lib/cmake/proj/proj-config.cmake` was manually confirmed as valid.

Expected behavior and actual behavior.

`GDAL` cannot link against arbitrary `proj` versions and will use `proj9` over any other `proj >=6`, regardless of how the search paths for cmake are constructed.

Specifically:
1. The documentation suggests that an arbitrary proj location can be specified via these cmake variables `PROJ_INCLUDE_DIR` and `PROJ_LIBRARY_RELEASE`
https://gdal.org/development/building_from_source.html#proj
2. However, these appear to only be used when searching for `proj` in a non-`CONFIG` mode
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/modules/packages/FindPROJ.cmake#L37
which only seems to happen when looking for `proj6`
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L378
3. Otherwise, the proj find logic uses a `CONFIG` search, and prioritizes `proj9` and `proj8` 
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L371

Therefore, the outcome is that arbitrary versions of proj>=6 cannot be used. 
For example, in the context of library dependency management like `spack`, if `proj7` is first in the cmake search paths, `GDAL` will pass over it, and use `proj 9`. This is problematic if a system `proj9` is used (e.g., from homebrew or apt) instead of a custom-built `proj`.

Below is a `find_pacakge` debug  trace from cmake where I was expecting a custom-built `proj7.2.1` to be used and linked against. Instead, `GDAL` linked against homebrew's `proj9`. The path `/Users/cmarsh/Documents/science/code/spack/opt/spack/darwin-ventura-m1/apple-clang-14.0.3/proj-7.2.1-ulvikgu6cuz3c2xwmuxifqlhnnkg6353/lib/cmake/proj/proj-config.cmake` was manually confirmed as valid.

Expected behavior and actual behavior.

`GDAL` cannot link against arbitrary `proj` versions and will use `proj9` over any other `proj >=6`, regardless of how the search paths for cmake are constructed.

Specifically:
1. The documentation suggests that an arbitrary proj location can be specified via these cmake variables `PROJ_INCLUDE_DIR` and `PROJ_LIBRARY_RELEASE`
https://gdal.org/development/building_from_source.html#proj
2. However, these appear to only be used when searching for `proj` in a non-`CONFIG` mode
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/modules/packages/FindPROJ.cmake#L37
which only seems to happen when looking for `proj6`
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L378
3. Otherwise, the proj find logic uses a `CONFIG` search, and prioritizes `proj9` and `proj8` 
https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/v3.8.0/cmake/helpers/CheckDependentLibraries.cmake#L371

Therefore, the outcome is that arbitrary versions of proj>=6 cannot be used. 
For example, in the context of library dependency management like `spack`, if `proj7` is first in the cmake search paths, `GDAL` will pass over it, and use `proj 9`. This is problematic if a system `proj9` is used (e.g., from homebrew or apt) instead of a custom-built `proj`.

Below is a `find_pacakge` debug  trace from cmake where I was expecting a custom-built `proj7.2.1` to be used and linked against. Instead, `GDAL` linked against homebrew's `proj9`. The path `/Users/cmarsh/Documents/science/code/spack/opt/spack/darwin-ventura-m1/apple-clang-14.0.3/proj-7.2.1-ulvikgu6cuz3c2xwmuxifqlhnnkg6353/lib/cmake/proj/proj-config.cmake` was manually confirmed as valid.

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.
- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [x] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [x] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [x] Build number is 0.
- [x] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [x] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [x] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the

new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team

-03T15:42:54Z"},"body":"<!--
Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team

What happened was that both repos were created at the same time. The lightly-utils repo passed, but the lightly repo failed because no lightly-utils binary existed yet. I just had to add a newer version of lightly (or bump the recipe to build version 1) to get lightly to pass.

There is an automated workflow to update the version and hash on every release, see https://github.com/conda-forge/lightly-feedstock/pull/1 for an example. It also suggests:

> If you want these PRs to be merged automatically, make an issue with `@conda-forge-admin, please add bot automerge` in the title and merge the resulting PR. This command will add our bot automerge feature to your feedstock.

The only work you would need to do is to update the dependencies when necessary.

We also have a [Spack recipe](https://github.com/spack/spack/blob/develop/var/spack/repos/builtin/packages/py-lightly/package.py) if you want to help maintain that. Unfortunately there's no automated workflow for that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/staged-recipes/issues/comments/1746481519/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.
- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [x] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [x] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [x] Build number is 0.
- [x] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [x] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [x] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

I'm unable to build numpy 1.26.1 with the new linker added in Xcode 15. However, adding the following allows the build to work:
```bash
export LDFLAGS=-Wl,-ld_classic
```

### Reproduce the code example:

* [build log](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/files/13046395/spack-build-out.txt)
* [build env](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/files/13046396/spack-build-env-mods.txt)


### Error message:

```shell
Run-time dependency scipy-openblas found: NO (tried pkgconfig, framework and cmake)
  WARNING: CMake Toolchain: Failed to determine CMake compilers state
  Run-time dependency openblas found: NO (tried pkgconfig, pkgconfig, pkgconfig and system)

  ../../numpy/meson.build:169:4: ERROR: Problem encountered: No BLAS library detected! Install one, or use the `allow-noblas` build option (note, this may be up to 100x slower for some linear algebra operations).
```


### Runtime information:

Numpy 1.26.1
Python 3.11.6
OpenBLAS 0.3.24

### Context for the issue:

Possibly related to https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19357

```bash

```

### Reproduce the code example:

* [build log](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/files/13046395/spack-build-out.txt)
* [build env](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/files/13046396/spack-build-env-mods.txt)


### Error message:

```shell
Run-time dependency scipy-openblas found: NO (tried pkgconfig, framework and cmake)
  WARNING: CMake Toolchain: Failed to determine CMake compilers state
  Run-time dependency openblas found: NO (tried pkgconfig, pkgconfig, pkgconfig and system)

  ../../numpy/meson.build:169:4: ERROR: Problem encountered: No BLAS library detected! Install one, or use the `allow-noblas` build option (note, this may be up to 100x slower for some linear algebra operations).
```


### Runtime information:

Numpy 1.26.1
Python 3.11.6
OpenBLAS 0.3.24

### Context for the issue:

Possibly related to https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19357

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

**Screenshots**

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

Fixed a typo in the `broken_barh` documentation: extend -> extent

## PR Checklist


**Documentation and Tests**

- [x] Has pytest style unit tests (and `pytest` passes)
- [x] Documentation is sphinx and numpydoc compliant (the docs should [build](https://matplotlib.org/devel/documenting_mpl.html#building-the-docs) without error).
- [x] New plotting related features are documented with examples.

**Release Notes**
- [x] New features are marked with a `.. versionadded::` directive in the docstring and documented in `doc/users/next_whats_new/`
- [x] API changes are marked with a `.. versionchanged::` directive in the docstring and documented in `doc/api/next_api_changes/`
- [x] Release notes conform with instructions in  `next_whats_new/README.rst` or `next_api_changes/README.rst`

* win_amd64
* manylinux_x86_64
* manylinux_aarch64
* macosx_arm64
* macosx_x86_64

Shouldn't you instead use something like:
```
    "scipy>=1.8,<1.9.2; python_version<='3.9'; sys_platform=='win32'",
    "scipy>=1.8",
```
Also, the lack of win32 wheels for scipy does not preclude users from building scipy from source, so I don't think it makes sense to prevent that use case either. Scikit-image doesn't even build wheels for win32, so why are we testing it?

I vote to either revert this commit (the files have moved around since then so `git revert` won't help) or implement the suggestion above. Happy to submit a PR if you want.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/comments/1450878366/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Re-enables the use of new scipy on old python. See https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/6567#issuecomment-1450878366 for context.

## Checklist

<!-- It's fine to submit PRs which are a work in progress! -->
<!-- But before they are merged, all PRs should provide: -->

- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/example.py)
- Gallery example in `./doc/examples` (new features only)
- Benchmark in `./benchmarks`, if your changes aren't covered by an
  existing benchmark
- Unit tests
- Clean style in [the spirit of PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/)
- Descriptive commit messages (see below)

<!-- For detailed information on these and other aspects see -->
<!-- the scikit-image contribution guidelines. -->
<!-- https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/contribute.html -->

## For reviewers

<!-- Don't remove the checklist below. -->

- Check that the PR title is short, concise, and will make sense 1 year
  later.
- Check that new functions are imported in corresponding `__init__.py`.
- Check that new features, API changes, and deprecations are mentioned in
  `doc/release/release_dev.rst`.
- There is a bot to help automate backporting a PR to an older branch. For
  example, to backport to v0.19.x after merging, add the following in a PR
  comment: `@meeseeksdev backport to v0.19.x`
- To run benchmarks on a PR, add the `run-benchmark` label. To rerun, the label
  can be removed and then added again. The benchmark output can be checked in
  the "Actions" tab.

Description

Re-enables the use of new scipy on old python. See https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/6567#issuecomment-1450878366 for context.

## Checklist

<!-- It's fine to submit PRs which are a work in progress! -->
<!-- But before they are merged, all PRs should provide: -->

- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/example.py)
- Gallery example in `./doc/examples` (new features only)
- Benchmark in `./benchmarks`, if your changes aren't covered by an
  existing benchmark
- Unit tests
- Clean style in [the spirit of PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/)
- Descriptive commit messages (see below)

<!-- For detailed information on these and other aspects see -->
<!-- the scikit-image contribution guidelines. -->
<!-- https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/contribute.html -->

## For reviewers

<!-- Don't remove the checklist below. -->

- Check that the PR title is short, concise, and will make sense 1 year
  later.
- Check that new functions are imported in corresponding `__init__.py`.
- Check that new features, API changes, and deprecations are mentioned in
  `doc/release/release_dev.rst`.
- There is a bot to help automate backporting a PR to an older branch. For
  example, to backport to v0.19.x after merging, add the following in a PR
  comment: `@meeseeksdev backport to v0.19.x`
- To run benchmarks on a PR, add the `run-benchmark` label. To rerun, the label
  can be removed and then added again. The benchmark output can be checked in
  the "Actions" tab.

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

First, like Conda, each package has to be updated manually, so it could be that one of the packages that had M1 issues hasn't been updated. If you know off the top of your head which packages had issues that would help a lot. Second, like Nix and Brew, Spack installs each package to its own separate installation prefix and (optionally) later symlinks them to a common shared environment. I believe we had issues with Jupyter plugins in the past that assumed everything was in the same directory, but I think we solved those via env vars. 

@rchoudhary uses pip and assures me that pip-installed Jupyter works for him, so I suspect you're right that this issue is Spack-specific and a newer version of a package may help. Just not sure which packages to try to update.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/comments/1480086223/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

the bug**

I'm experiencing connection/kernel issues with my local copy of Jupyter. I'm not sure if this is the right place to report this, but I encounter the issue with both `jupyter lab` and `jupyter notebook` so it may be an issue with a common core/server dependency.

**To Reproduce**

1. Launch Jupyter with `jupyter lab` or `jupyter notebook`
2. Create a new notebook with a simple `print('hello world')` line
3. Try to run this line
4. Nothing happens, errors in log

**Expected behavior**

I would Jupyter to find and use the kernel and run my code properly.

## Checklist

<!-- It's fine to submit PRs which are a work in progress! -->
<!-- But before they are merged, all PRs should provide: -->

- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/example.py)
- Gallery example in `./doc/examples` (new features only)
- Benchmark in `./benchmarks`, if your changes aren't covered by an
  existing benchmark
- Unit tests
- Clean style in [the spirit of PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/)
- Descriptive commit messages (see below)

<!-- For detailed information on these and other aspects see -->
<!-- the scikit-image contribution guidelines. -->
<!-- https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/contribute.html -->

## For reviewers

<!-- Don't remove the checklist below. -->

- Check that the PR title is short, concise, and will make sense 1 year
  later.
- Check that new functions are imported in corresponding `__init__.py`.
- Check that new features, API changes, and deprecations are mentioned in
  `doc/release/release_dev.rst`.
- There is a bot to help automate backporting a PR to an older branch. For
  example, to backport to v0.19.x after merging, add the following in a PR
  comment: `@meeseeksdev backport to v0.19.x`
- To run benchmarks on a PR, add the `run-benchmark` label. To rerun, the label
  can be removed and then added again. The benchmark output can be checked in
  the "Actions" tab.

@jarrodmillman @stefanv Re-enables the use of new scipy on old python. See https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/6567#issuecomment-1450878366 for context.

## Checklist

<!-- It's fine to submit PRs which are a work in progress! -->
<!-- But before they are merged, all PRs should provide: -->

- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/example.py)
- Gallery example in `./doc/examples` (new features only)
- Benchmark in `./benchmarks`, if your changes aren't covered by an
  existing benchmark
- Unit tests
- Clean style in [the spirit of PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/)
- Descriptive commit messages (see below)

<!-- For detailed information on these and other aspects see -->
<!-- the scikit-image contribution guidelines. -->
<!-- https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/contribute.html -->

## For reviewers

<!-- Don't remove the checklist below. -->

- Check that the PR title is short, concise, and will make sense 1 year
  later.
- Check that new functions are imported in corresponding `__init__.py`.
- Check that new features, API changes, and deprecations are mentioned in
  `doc/release/release_dev.rst`.
- There is a bot to help automate backporting a PR to an older branch. For
  example, to backport to v0.19.x after merging, add the following in a PR
  comment: `@meeseeksdev backport to v0.19.x`
- To run benchmarks on a PR, add the `run-benchmark` label. To rerun, the label
  can be removed and then added again. The benchmark output can be checked in
  the "Actions" tab.

#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.
Since we do not depend on `numpy.distutils` anymore, I think it's safe to raise the upper bound for `setuptools`. 

#### Any other comments?
The alternative is to not have an upper bound at all.

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

We're trying to update the [TorchGeo feedstock](https://github.com/conda-forge/torchgeo-feedstock/pull/10) but we're currently hitting the following issue with `pip check`:
```
pytorch-lightning 1.8.1 has requirement lightning-utilities==0.3.*, but you have lightning-utilities 0.6.0.post0.
```
The recipe listed here actually says `>=0.3` so I'm not sure where it's getting `==0.3.*` from. Maybe the binary on conda-forge wasn't updated because the tests failed?

Also, 1.8.1 is quite old. Are there any plans to update to 1.9.0? Happy to submit a PR to do this but there are already a few auto-opened PRs that haven't been addressed so I don't want to do this if this recipe isn't being maintained anymore.

 If the version is out of date, please `create an issue or pull request <https://github.com/Microsoft/vcpkg>`__ on the vcpkg repository.
 
+Spack
+................................................................................
+
+Spack is a package management tool designed to support multiple versions and
+configurations of software on a wide variety of platforms and environments.
+It was designed for large supercomputing centers. Spack builds packages from
+sources, and allows tweaking their configurations.
+
+You can find information about GDAL in Spack at
+https://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/package_list.html#gdal
+
+For the default GDAL build with a reduced number of drivers:
+
+::
+
+    git clone -c feature.manyFiles=true https://github.com/spack/spack.git
+    cd spack/bin
+    ./spack install gdal
+
+For a build with netcdf driver enabled (cf https://github.com/spack/spack/issues/12586 for the explanation of the ^mpich~fortran workaround)
+
+::
+
+    ./spack install gdal +netcdf ^mpich~fortran","path":"doc/source/download.rst","position":53,"original_position":53,"commit_id":"70e5cf713cef796bea94240c8b44a75bc7af7bac","original_commit_id":"70e5cf713cef796bea94240c8b44a75bc7af7bac","user":{"login":"adamjstewart","id":12021217,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMDIxMjE3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12021217?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart","html_url":"https://github.com/adamjstewart","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Is this workaround actually needed? {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/OSGeo/gdal/pulls/comments/1020637195","pull_request_review_id":1177967114,"id":1020637195,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAF3Q3c481bAL","diff_hunk":"@@ -291,6 +296,31 @@ You can download and install gdal using the vcpkg dependency manager:
 
 If the version is out of date, please `create an issue or pull request <https://github.com/Microsoft/vcpkg>`__ on the vcpkg repository.
 
+Spack
+................................................................................
+
+Spack is a package management tool designed to support multiple versions and
+configurations of software on a wide variety of platforms and environments.
+It was designed for large supercomputing centers. Spack builds packages from
+sources, and allows tweaking their configurations.
+
+You can find information about GDAL in Spack at
+https://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/package_list.html#gdal
+
+For the default GDAL build with a reduced number of drivers:
+
+::
+
+    git clone -c feature.manyFiles=true https://github.com/spack/spack.git
+    cd spack/bin
+    ./spack install gdal
+
+For a build with netcdf driver enabled (cf https://github.com/spack/spack/issues/12586 for the explanation of the ^mpich~fortran workaround)
+
+::
+
+    ./spack install gdal +netcdf ^mpich~fortran","path":"doc/source/download.rst","position":53,"original_position":53,"commit_id":"70e5cf713cef796bea94240c8b44a75bc7af7bac","original_commit_id":"70e5cf713cef796bea94240c8b44a75bc7af7bac","user":{"login":"adamjstewart","id":12021217,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMDIxMjE3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12021217?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart","html_url":"https://github.com/adamjstewart","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adamjstewart/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Can you open a new issue or comment on the old issue? Our pins are already fairly relaxed. The minimum is tested in CI, and can't go any lower. The maximum is based on [semver](https://semver.org/). Since we aren't able to test new major releases, which are used only when backwards incompatible changes are made, we set an upper bound on the newest major version that has been tested.

Unfortunately, package managers like pip and conda do not allow you to change the version bounds of historical releases. [Spack](https://spack.io) does, but not everyone uses Spack, so we try to be conservative about releases we've never tested before. This was needed because new major releases of libraries like pillow, and new minor releases of beta software like torchmetrics and radiant-mlhub, have resulted in incompatibilities on multiple occasions. If you recall, when protobuf 4 was released, pretty much all libraries that depend on protobuf broke since they didn't pin their dependencies to be `<4` like they should have. It took months for these libraries to ship new releases to fix this mistake. I would rather avoid a repeat of https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/issues/10051.

> The geo ecosystem is really fragile and annoying, and your package makes it even more so.

I would argue that relaxing our pins even further (aka removing them) would make the geo ecosystem significantly more fragile, since users could easily install versions of dependencies that simply do not work. I've never encountered any issues installing torchgeo, although I don't personally use conda.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/tensorboard-feedstock/issues/comments/1242343664/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'm [biased](https://github.com/spack/spack/graphs/contributors) 😄 

> You should check out conda btw, you may end up in love

I used to use conda for ~5 years but eventually gave up. Years of fighting against ABI compatibility issues every time I mixed channels or upgraded packages was a strong deterrent. Conda also lacks many of the features I need, including:

* optional dependencies
* optional build flags
* control over compilers
* control over MPI and BLAS/LAPACK libraries

With Spack I can do something like:
```console
$ spack install py-torchgeo+datasets %gcc@9.3.0 ^py-torch@1.11.0+cuda+distributed+mpi cuda_arch=80 ^openmpi
```
This builds TorchGeo, with optional dataset-specific dependencies, using PyTorch 1.11, with CUDA and MPI support, using the OpenMPI backend, all with the GCC 9.3.0 compiler. 

Of course, this is only possible because Spack builds everything from source, and there are many disadvantages of from-source package managers (especially build time). I don't want to turn this into a discussion of which package manager is better (especially in an away game) since they serve very different purposes. Conda is more targeted to the average user, while Spack is more targeted to supercomputing environments where microarchitecture-specific optimizations are important for optimal performance.

Thanks for #67, I'll try removing our protobuf pin and see if pip check now passes. I'll also do what I can to encourage TF/tensorboard devs to offer support for protobuf 3.20. I think 4.21 support would require work on their part, but 3.20 support should work fine.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/tensorboard-feedstock/issues/comments/1242772456/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

is simply documenting the status quo and putting an earlier check for it (rather than erroring out with an AttributeError at runtime), so no changelog.

Also, technically, only the gtk backends need pycairo 1.14 (the other backends can stick to 1.11), but 1.14 is old enough (2017) that it doesn't seem worth the complication of having two different bounds.

Closes #23827.

I'm trying to update matplotlib's build recipe in the [Spack](https://spack.io) package manager: https://github.com/spack/spack/pull/32697. However, I'm seeing a build issue relating to setuptools_scm eggs with matplotlib 3.6.0.

### Code for reproduction

```console
$ git clone https://github.com/adamjstewart/spack.git
$ cd spack
$ git switch packages/py-matplotlib
$ . share/spack/setup-env.sh
$ spack install -v py-matplotlib
```


### Actual outcome

When Spack tries to build matplotlib 3.6.0 from source, it encounters the following build error:

I'm guessing what's happening here is that the setuptools_scm version we have installed is incompatible with the setuptools_scm egg in the source directory, but I have no idea where that egg would be coming from. Matplotlib 3.5.3 builds fine, did anything change in 3.6.0 w.r.t. setuptools_scm/eggs?

### Expected outcome

I would expect matplotlib 3.6.0 to build fine, just like 3.5.3.

PR Summary
first pr, happy to add/make any necessary changes
closes #23487

The changed test in `test_colorbar.py` was related removing a colorbar from a scatter plot. I don't think the `cmap` there was necessary to test this. And running the tests on my own revealed the `cmap` wasn't changing the color of the scatter plot anyways

When making an animation of a scatter plot, if you don't set `c` (the color value parameter) when initializing the artist, the color settings are ignored.

### Code for reproduction

```python
import matplotlib.animation as animation
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
pts = ax.scatter([], [], cmap="gray")
ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)

def update(i):
    pts.set_offsets(np.random.rand(100, 2))
    pts.set_array(np.random.rand(100))

ani = animation.FuncAnimation(fig, func=update, frames=range(10))
plt.show()
```


### Actual outcome

Even though `cmap="gray"` is passed to `scatter`, the points use the default "viridis" color map.

### Expected outcome

I would expect the points to use the "gray" color map.

### Additional information

If you modify the above code to use:
```python
pts = ax.scatter([], [], c=[], cmap="gray")
```
it works as expected. It seems like all color-related settings, including cmap, vmin, vmax, etc. are discarded unless `c` is given during the first call to `scatter`. 

This workaround (passing an empty `c`) isn't problematic, but I found the default behavior quite unintuitive and it took me the better part of a day to figure out what was happening, so I figured it would be worth reporting.

Possible solutions:

* Raise an error/warning if `cmap`/`vmin`/`vmax` are given but `c` is not
* Store parameters like `cmap`/`vmin`/`vmax` even if they aren't immediately used

These changes should probably happen in `_parse_scatter_color_args` in `lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py`. According to `git blame`, @timhoffm @anntzer wrote most of this logic.

### Operating system

macOS 12.4

### Matplotlib Version

3.5.2

### Matplotlib Backend

MacOSX

### Python version

3.9.13

### Jupyter version

N/A

1. These strings will be concatenated without spaces, so your message will look like "vmaxare being" and "mapping,try passing". Just add spaces at the end or beginning of each string.
2. This current message is appropriate for my scenario in #23487 but more generally I think we should warn any time someone tries to pass one of these parameters without `c`. So maybe something generic like `"'c' not given, 'cmap', 'norm', 'vmin', and 'vmax' will be ignored"`. Since PNG isn't listed as a required dependency, I would expect to be able to build GDAL without PNG. However, CMake errors out with the following error message even if I use `-DGDAL_USE_PNG=OFF`:
```
CMake Error at gdal.cmake:478 (target_link_libraries):
  Target "GDAL" links to:

    PNG::PNG

  but the target was not found.  Possible reasons include:

    * There is a typo in the target name.
    * A find_package call is missing for an IMPORTED target.
    * An ALIAS target is missing.

Call Stack (most recent call first):
  CMakeLists.txt:218 (include)
```

## Steps to reproduce the problem.

```console
$ git clone https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal.git
$ cd gdal
$ cmake . -DGDAL_USE_PNG=OFF
```

## Operating system

macOS 12.4 (Apple M1 Pro)

## GDAL version and provenance

The 3.5.0 version built from source with Spack.

Full build logs:

* [build log](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/files/8983634/spack-build-out.txt)
* [build env](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/files/8983635/spack-build-env-mods.txt)

Comment:

In `environment.yml`, if we specify conda-forge `pytorch` as a dependency, how does conda select between `pytorch-cpu` and `pytorch-gpu`?  Currently there seems to be 3 pytorch repos in conda-forge i.e. `pytorch`, `pytorch-cpu` and `pytorch-gpu` but only one feedstock i.e. this one.

I use [Spack](https://spack.io) and the spec model is much more powerful so you can say things like:
```console
$ spack install py-torch@1.11.0 +cuda cuda_arch=60 ^cuda@11.7.0 %gcc@12.1.0
$ spack install py-torch@master +rocm -nnpack -qnnpack -xnnpack
```
and build specific versions of PyTorch with or without certain features, certain versions of dependencies, and certain compilers. I think the most control you have in conda is:
```console
$ conda install pytorch-gpu=1.11.0
```
and hope that that's all you care about.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pytorch-cpu-feedstock/issues/comments/1133942152/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
